fields.name: cramps
fields.description: Uterine contractions driven by prostaglandins to expel the uterine lining. Primary dysmenorrhea has no underlying cause; secondary is linked to conditions like endometriosis.
tips: Apply a heat pad to the lower abdomen
Ibuprofen is more effective than paracetamol as it blocks prostaglandins
Light walking increases blood flow and reduces cramping
Magnesium supplementation can reduce severity over time

fields.name: heavy bleeding
fields.description: Peak flow typically occurs on days 1–2 and tapers toward the end of the period. Losing more than 80ml per cycle is clinically defined as menorrhagia.
tips: Track flow level daily using the app
Consult a doctor if soaking more than one pad or tampon per hour for several hours
Iron-rich foods help replenish iron lost through blood
Consider period underwear or a menstrual cup for overnight protection

fields.name: lower back pain
fields.description: Referred pain from uterine contractions that radiate to the lower back and sacral region. The same prostaglandins that cause cramps trigger this pain.
tips: Apply heat to the lower back
Child's pose and gentle yoga stretches help
A TENS machine can provide relief
Ibuprofen addresses the prostaglandin source

fields.name: fatigue
fields.description: Blood loss reduces circulating iron and hemoglobin, lowering oxygen delivery to cells. Estrogen and progesterone are also at their lowest, reducing energy-regulating neurotransmitters.
tips: Rest as needed — this is not laziness, it is physiology
Eat iron-rich foods such as spinach, lentils, and red meat
Vitamin C alongside iron sources enhances absorption
Stay hydrated as dehydration compounds fatigue

fields.name: headache
fields.description: Triggered by the sharp drop in estrogen that occurs just before and during menstruation. Estrogen withdrawal lowers serotonin and affects blood vessel dilation in the brain.
tips: Stay well hydrated — dehydration worsens hormonal headaches
Avoid caffeine withdrawal during this phase which can trigger rebound headaches
Magnesium glycinate supplementation is evidence-based for menstrual migraines
A cold compress on the forehead and dark room rest help

fields.name: bloating
fields.description: Prostaglandins cause water retention and slow GI motility. The uterus is also slightly enlarged during menstruation adding to the sensation of abdominal fullness.
tips: Reduce sodium and processed food intake
Avoid carbonated drinks
Herbal teas such as peppermint and fennel aid digestion
Light exercise reduces water retention and stimulates bowel movement

fields.name: nausea
fields.description: High prostaglandin levels can affect the gastrointestinal tract causing nausea, especially on heavy flow days when prostaglandins are highest.
tips: Eat small frequent meals rather than large ones
Ginger tea or crystallised ginger is evidence-based for nausea
Avoid greasy, spicy or heavily processed foods
Rest in a well-ventilated area if nausea is severe

fields.name: diarrhea
fields.description: Prostaglandins act on smooth muscle throughout the body including the intestines. Excess prostaglandins spill into the bloodstream causing gut contractions and loose stools.
tips: Avoid high-fibre foods on heavy flow days
Stay hydrated to replace lost fluids and electrolytes
The BRAT diet (banana, rice, applesauce, toast) is gentle on the gut
Ibuprofen reduces prostaglandins and may help GI symptoms too

fields.name: breast tenderness
fields.description: Residual tenderness from the luteal phase progesterone surge that typically improves once bleeding begins and hormone levels drop.
tips: Wear a well-fitting supportive bra including at night if severe
Reduce caffeine intake which is linked to cyclic breast pain
Evening primrose oil has some evidence for reducing cyclic mastalgia
A warm compress provides relief

fields.name: mood swings
fields.description: The collapse of estrogen and progesterone at the start of menstruation disrupts serotonin, dopamine and GABA signalling causing rapid shifts between irritability, sadness and brief moments of relief.
tips: Gentle aerobic exercise is the most effective natural serotonin booster
Prioritise sleep — sleep deprivation magnifies hormonal mood effects
Omega-3 fatty acids support neurotransmitter function
Avoid scheduling high-stakes social or professional events on day 1–2

fields.name: low mood
fields.description: The simultaneous crash of estrogen and progesterone at menstruation onset affects serotonin receptor sensitivity and dopamine availability causing a low, flat or sad mood.
tips: Social support and connection genuinely help even when withdrawal feels tempting
Sunlight exposure raises serotonin — even a short walk outdoors helps
Avoid alcohol which is a CNS depressant and worsens hormonal low mood
This typically lifts naturally as estrogen begins rising in the follicular phase

fields.name: pale skin
fields.description: Significant blood loss causes a temporary drop in hemoglobin and red blood cell count causing pallor, particularly noticeable around the lips and fingernails.
tips: Eat iron-rich foods: red meat, spinach, lentils, tofu, fortified cereals
Pair iron sources with vitamin C to enhance absorption
Consider a short-term iron supplement if periods are consistently heavy
Consult a doctor if pallor is severe or accompanied by breathlessness

fields.name: pelvic pressure
fields.description: Uterine contractions and increased pelvic blood flow during menstruation create a sensation of heaviness, fullness or pressure in the lower abdomen and pelvis.
tips: Rest with legs slightly elevated
Warm baths relax pelvic muscles and relieve pressure
A heat pad on the lower abdomen is effective
Child's pose yoga stretch decompresses the pelvis

fields.name: appetite changes
fields.description: Nausea, cramps and low mood during menstruation suppress appetite in some people while the hormonal reset triggers increased hunger in others.
tips: Eat nourishing whole foods regardless of whether appetite is high or low
Do not skip meals — blood sugar instability worsens mood and fatigue
Iron and B-vitamin rich foods are particularly helpful in this phase
Listen to your body's signals rather than forcing a routine

fields.name: clotting
fields.description: Clots form when blood pools in the uterus faster than anticoagulants can break it down. Small clots are normal during heavy flow. Large clots may indicate fibroids or other conditions.
tips: Clots smaller than a 50p coin are generally normal
Consistently large clots with very heavy flow warrant a GP visit
Staying hydrated keeps blood slightly less viscous
Tracking clot size over multiple cycles is useful information for your doctor

fields.name: iron deficiency symptoms
fields.description: Repeated blood loss through menstruation is the most common cause of iron deficiency in people with periods. Symptoms include fatigue, brain fog, cold hands and brittle nails.
tips: Eat iron-rich foods throughout the cycle not just during the period
Avoid tea and coffee with meals as tannins inhibit iron absorption
A full blood count from your GP can confirm iron status
Iron bisglycinate supplements cause fewer GI side effects than ferrous sulphate

fields.name: chills
fields.description: Prostaglandins and blood loss can disrupt thermoregulation causing episodes of feeling cold or shivery, particularly on the heaviest flow days.
tips: Layer clothing for easy temperature adjustment
Warm drinks and soups help maintain core temperature
A hot water bottle provides comfort
If accompanied by fever above 38°C consult a doctor as this may indicate infection

fields.name: fever
fields.description: A mild low-grade temperature can occasionally accompany heavy periods. A significant fever during menstruation may indicate pelvic inflammatory disease or infection and requires medical attention.
tips: Monitor temperature with a thermometer
Fever above 38°C during menstruation warrants same-day medical attention
Stay hydrated
Do not ignore fever with pelvic pain — this combination can indicate a serious infection

fields.name: sensitivity to light
fields.description: Menstrual migraines and tension headaches are commonly accompanied by photophobia — a heightened sensitivity to light that makes bright environments painful.
tips: Rest in a darkened room
Wear sunglasses outdoors or in bright indoor environments
Reduce screen brightness and use night mode
Address the underlying headache with hydration and pain relief

fields.name: sensitivity to sound
fields.description: Like photophobia, sound sensitivity accompanies menstrual migraines and headaches due to heightened neurological sensitivity driven by estrogen withdrawal.
tips: Use earplugs or noise-cancelling headphones
Rest in a quiet environment
Avoid loud social environments on migraine days
Address the underlying headache first

fields.name: dizziness
fields.description: Blood loss and the associated drop in blood pressure during menstruation can cause lightheadedness or dizziness, particularly when standing up quickly.
tips: Stand up slowly from sitting or lying positions
Stay well hydrated as dehydration compounds low blood pressure
Eat regular meals to maintain blood sugar
If dizziness is severe or accompanied by fainting seek medical advice

fields.name: abdominal pain
fields.description: Diffuse abdominal pain associated with uterine contractions, GI changes driven by prostaglandins, and generalised pelvic congestion during menstruation.
tips: Heat therapy on the abdomen is highly effective
Ibuprofen targets the prostaglandin cause
Avoid foods that cause gas or bloating
Gentle massage of the lower abdomen in circular motions can help

fields.name: pelvic pain
fields.description: Pain in the pelvic cavity from uterine contractions, increased blood flow and pelvic congestion. Persistent or worsening pelvic pain across cycles may indicate endometriosis or fibroids.
tips: Heat therapy is first-line relief
NSAIDs like ibuprofen are more effective than paracetamol
Consult a doctor if pelvic pain is worsening cycle on cycle
Track severity and character of pain to help a doctor assess

fields.name: back pain
fields.description: General lower back pain associated with menstrual cramps, uterine referred pain and ligament laxity from hormonal changes.
tips: Heat therapy applied to lower back
Gentle stretching and yoga
Anti-inflammatory pain relief
Avoid sitting in one position for long periods

fields.name: joint pain
fields.description: Systemic prostaglandin release and low-grade inflammation during menstruation can cause generalised joint aching similar to the joint pain of a mild flu.
tips: Gentle movement is better than complete rest for joint pain
An anti-inflammatory diet rich in omega-3 fatty acids helps
Warm baths ease joint stiffness
Ibuprofen addresses both prostaglandins and joint inflammation

fields.name: muscle aches
fields.description: Prostaglandins cause systemic inflammation that manifests as generalised muscle soreness, often similar in feel to the body aches of illness.
tips: Light stretching maintains circulation and reduces stiffness
Warm baths with Epsom salts are soothing
Magnesium supplementation reduces muscle cramping and soreness
Gentle yoga is more restorative than complete rest

fields.name: nasal congestion
fields.description: Estrogen affects mucous membrane tissue throughout the body including the nasal passages. Its drop during menstruation can paradoxically increase congestion in some people.
tips: Saline nasal spray is safe and effective
Stay hydrated
A humidifier in the bedroom helps
Nasal strips can improve airflow at night

fields.name: sore throat
fields.description: Immune function fluctuates with the menstrual cycle. Some research suggests reduced immune defence during menstruation making minor infections more likely.
tips: Warm drinks with honey and lemon soothe the throat
Gargling with warm salt water reduces inflammation
Consult a doctor if severe or persistent
Prioritise rest to support immune function

fields.name: eye strain
fields.description: Fatigue and hormonal headaches during menstruation increase sensitivity in and around the eyes, making screen use and reading more fatiguing.
tips: Take regular breaks from screens using the 20-20-20 rule
A warm compress on closed eyes relieves strain
Reduce screen brightness
Rest the eyes in a dark room if headache accompanies the strain

fields.name: vision changes
fields.description: Menstrual migraines can cause visual auras including zigzag lines, blind spots or temporary blurring. Fluid retention can also subtly affect corneal curvature.
tips: Rest in a dark quiet room if a visual aura precedes a migraine
Contact lens wearers may find glasses more comfortable during this phase
Consult a doctor if vision changes are new, severe or persistent

fields.name: ear ringing
fields.description: Fluid retention and changes in blood pressure during menstruation can cause brief episodes of tinnitus in people who are susceptible.
tips: Consult a doctor if tinnitus is persistent or worsening
Avoid loud environments
Reducing salt intake can reduce fluid retention that contributes to tinnitus

fields.name: rising energy
fields.description: Rising estrogen boosts serotonin and dopamine availability and increases mitochondrial efficiency. Physical and cognitive energy progressively improves from around day 6 onwards.
tips: This is an ideal phase for starting new projects or habits
High-intensity exercise yields better results and feels easier now
Capitalise on natural motivation for tasks you've been avoiding
Social energy is high — schedule important meetings and events

fields.name: improved mood
fields.description: Rising estrogen increases the sensitivity of serotonin receptors and promotes dopamine release, creating a progressive improvement in mood, optimism and emotional resilience.
tips: Schedule demanding social situations and difficult conversations in this phase
Your emotional resilience is highest now — use it strategically
Reflect on what went well in the previous cycle

fields.name: clear skin
fields.description: Estrogen promotes collagen synthesis and reduces sebum production. Skin is often at its clearest and most luminous in the follicular phase.
tips: A good phase to introduce new skincare treatments as skin is less reactive
Estrogen improves wound healing so skin recovers faster from extractions
Stay hydrated for maximum skin benefit

fields.name: increased libido
fields.description: Rising estrogen and testosterone in the lead-up to ovulation increase sexual desire. This is biologically driven by the body preparing for peak fertility.
tips: This is the body's natural preparation for the fertile window
Communication with a partner about cycle-related changes in libido supports relationship wellbeing

fields.name: cervical mucus changes
fields.description: As ovulation approaches, cervical mucus transitions from dry or sticky (early follicular) through creamy and white to clear and stretchy. Tracking this is one of the most reliable fertility awareness methods.
tips: Egg-white consistency cervical mucus signals approaching ovulation — peak fertility is 1–2 days away
Check mucus consistency by observing toilet paper or using clean fingers
Useful for both those trying to conceive and those avoiding pregnancy

fields.name: light spotting
fields.description: Light brown or pink discharge in the first couple of days after menstruation ends. This is old blood clearing from the uterus and is normal.
tips: Brown discharge is oxidised old blood and is completely normal
Should resolve within 1–2 days
If fresh red spotting persists beyond day 7 consult a doctor

fields.name: mild breast sensitivity
fields.description: Rising estrogen causes mild breast tissue proliferation and fluid retention in the breast, leading to a slight increase in sensitivity or fullness in the late follicular phase.
tips: A well-fitting sports bra reduces discomfort during exercise
This is much milder than luteal phase breast tenderness and should not be alarming

fields.name: improved concentration
fields.description: Estrogen enhances verbal memory, processing speed, cognitive flexibility and executive function. This is one of the most cognitively sharp phases of the cycle.
tips: Schedule cognitively demanding work, presentations and creative projects now
Memory and learning are enhanced — ideal for studying or acquiring new skills
You may find it easier to have clarity on decisions that felt harder in the luteal phase

fields.name: increased sociability
fields.description: Higher estrogen and dopamine increase confidence, verbal fluency and the drive to connect socially. People often feel more extroverted and articulate in this phase.
tips: Great time for networking events, job interviews, first dates, and social commitments
Your ability to read social cues and communicate clearly is enhanced
Use this energy to address relationship conversations you've been putting off

fields.name: mild bloating resolution
fields.description: As prostaglandins drop and estrogen rises, the water retention and GI sluggishness of menstruation resolves. Many people notice their abdomen feels flatter.
tips: This is a natural resolution — no specific action needed
Staying hydrated and reducing salt accelerates the process

fields.name: appetite normalisation
fields.description: Estrogen has a mild appetite-suppressing effect and stabilises blood sugar regulation, making hunger cues feel more consistent and easier to manage.
tips: A good phase to establish healthy eating habits as cravings are lower
Regular meals are easier to maintain in this phase
Use this phase to meal prep for the luteal phase when cravings are stronger

fields.name: light headaches
fields.description: Some people experience brief mild headaches in the early follicular phase as estrogen begins rising rapidly from its lowest point. These are distinct from menstrual migraines.
tips: Stay hydrated
These typically resolve within 1–2 days as estrogen stabilises
Paracetamol or ibuprofen if needed

fields.name: hyperactivity
fields.description: The peak of estrogen and dopamine in the late follicular phase can in some people tip into restless hyperactive energy — a feeling of being 'wired' or unable to slow down.
tips: Channel energy into exercise or creative work
Avoid caffeine if feeling overly wired
Mindfulness or journaling can ground scattered energy

fields.name: mittelschmerz
fields.description: One-sided lower abdominal pain occurring when the dominant follicle ruptures and releases the egg. Can range from a brief twinge to several hours of cramping. Typically alternates sides each cycle.
tips: A heating pad on the affected side helps
Ibuprofen is effective if pain is significant
Pain alternating sides each cycle is reassuring that this is ovulatory
Seek medical attention if pain is severe, one-sided and accompanied by fever — this could indicate a ruptured cyst

fields.name: egg white cervical mucus
fields.description: At peak fertility, cervical mucus becomes clear, slippery and highly elastic — resembling raw egg white. This is the most reliable natural sign that ovulation is imminent or occurring.
tips: This is the peak fertility window — sperm can survive up to 5 days in this mucus
The mucus should stretch 3–5cm between fingers without breaking
Tracking this alongside BBT gives the most complete fertility picture

fields.name: ovulation spotting
fields.description: Brief light pink or brown spotting caused by the rupture of the follicle wall. The small amount of blood from the follicle can appear as light spotting lasting 1–2 days.
tips: Lasts only 1–2 days maximum
Normal and no cause for concern
Brown spotting indicates old blood from the follicle rupture
If heavy or prolonged consult a doctor

fields.name: peak libido
fields.description: Testosterone and estrogen both peak around ovulation, creating the highest point of sexual desire in the cycle. This is a biologically driven mechanism to encourage reproduction.
tips: This is a natural biological phenomenon
Communicating with a partner about cycle-related libido changes supports mutual understanding

fields.name: heightened senses
fields.description: Research suggests that smell, vision and taste are subtly but measurably enhanced around ovulation. Estrogen and LH affect olfactory sensitivity in particular.
tips: Many people notice they are more sensitive to perfumes and body odours around this time
Food may taste more intense
This is a fascinating aspect of cycle biology worth paying attention to

fields.name: elevated basal body temperature
fields.description: After ovulation, the corpus luteum releases progesterone which has a thermogenic effect, raising basal body temperature by 0.2–0.5°C. This sustained rise confirms that ovulation has occurred.
tips: Measure BBT immediately upon waking before getting out of bed, speaking, or drinking
Use a basal thermometer which measures to two decimal places
A sustained rise of at least 0.2°C maintained for 3 or more days confirms ovulation
BBT charting combined with cervical mucus observation is highly effective for fertility awareness

fields.name: peak energy and confidence
fields.description: The convergence of peak estrogen, testosterone and LH around ovulation creates the highest point of physical energy, social confidence and emotional wellbeing in the cycle.
tips: Schedule important presentations, interviews, dates and social events around this window
Physical performance peaks — ideal for personal bests in sport or exercise
Creative confidence is also highest now

fields.name: mild nausea
fields.description: The LH surge and rapid hormonal shifts around ovulation can cause brief, mild nausea in some people. This is distinct from the heavier nausea of menstruation or early pregnancy.
tips: Brief and self-resolving within hours
Eat a small light snack if nausea is bothersome
Ginger tea or ginger chews help

fields.name: increased vaginal discharge
fields.description: Peak estrogen stimulates maximum cervical mucus production just before ovulation. This significant increase in clear, watery or egg-white discharge is a reliable ovulation sign.
tips: A noticeable increase in clear odourless discharge is a reliable sign of approaching ovulation
Should not be confused with infection — ovulatory discharge is clear and odourless
Panty liners can be used for comfort

fields.name: breast tenderness ovulation
fields.description: The rapid hormonal shifts of the LH surge can cause brief breast sensitivity lasting 1–2 days around ovulation, distinct from the more prolonged luteal phase tenderness.
tips: Brief and self-resolving
Supportive bra helps
Distinguish from the more persistent luteal phase tenderness which starts after ovulation

fields.name: heat sensitivity
fields.description: The rise in basal body temperature after ovulation increases sensitivity to environmental heat. Some notice feeling warmer than usual for several days around ovulation.
tips: Stay hydrated as body temperature is elevated
Wear breathable fabrics
Avoid overheating during exercise

fields.name: food cravings
fields.description: Falling serotonin in the late luteal phase triggers intense cravings for carbohydrates and sugar, which transiently boost serotonin. Cravings for chocolate specifically may relate to magnesium depletion.
tips: Complex carbohydrates such as oats, sweet potato and wholegrain bread are a healthier way to raise serotonin
Small frequent meals prevent the blood sugar crashes that intensify cravings
Dark chocolate satisfies chocolate cravings while providing magnesium
Magnesium supplementation may reduce the intensity of cravings over time

fields.name: acne breakout
fields.description: Progesterone increases sebum production and causes pore enlargement. Bacteria proliferate in excess sebum leading to inflammatory acne, typically on the chin, jawline and cheeks.
tips: Double cleanse in the evening to remove the day's excess sebum
Salicylic acid (BHA) is particularly effective for hormonal acne
Avoid touching your face which transfers bacteria
Consistency in skincare routine matters more than adding more products
Speak to a dermatologist if hormonal acne is severe

fields.name: water retention
fields.description: Progesterone activates aldosterone, a hormone that signals the kidneys to retain sodium and water. This causes temporary weight gain and puffiness, particularly in the abdomen, face and fingers.
tips: Paradoxically increasing water intake helps flush retained sodium and water
Reduce sodium intake from processed and packaged foods
Gentle exercise promotes lymphatic drainage and reduces puffiness
Avoid standing for long periods which worsens lower limb swelling

fields.name: anxiety
fields.description: Fluctuating progesterone metabolites affect GABA-A receptors in the brain — the same receptors targeted by anxiety medication. Simultaneously falling estrogen reduces serotonin, compounding anxious feelings.
tips: Magnesium glycinate is the most evidence-based supplement for PMS-related anxiety
Limit caffeine which amplifies anxiety in the luteal phase
Regular aerobic exercise is as effective as medication for mild-to-moderate anxiety
Diaphragmatic breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system and relieves acute anxiety
If anxiety is severe and cyclical consider speaking to a doctor about PMDD

fields.name: insomnia
fields.description: Despite progesterone's overall sedative effect, its fluctuations in the late luteal phase combined with physical discomfort, anxiety and elevated body temperature can significantly disrupt sleep architecture.
tips: Maintain a consistent sleep and wake time throughout the cycle
Keep the bedroom cool — the elevated BBT of the luteal phase makes heat more disruptive
Avoid alcohol which worsens sleep quality despite feeling sedating
Magnesium glycinate at bedtime promotes deeper sleep
Avoid screens for 60 minutes before bed

fields.name: constipation
fields.description: Progesterone relaxes smooth muscle throughout the body, including the intestinal walls. This slows peristalsis — the wave-like contractions that move food through the digestive tract — causing sluggish bowel movements.
tips: Increase dietary fibre from vegetables, fruit and wholegrains
Stay well hydrated — fibre requires water to work
Regular physical activity is one of the most effective natural remedies for constipation
Prunes, flaxseed and kiwi fruit are evidence-based for bowel regularity
Do not ignore the urge to go — delaying makes constipation worse

fields.name: reduced concentration
fields.description: Falling estrogen in the late luteal phase reduces cognitive sharpness, verbal fluency and working memory. This is a physiological change, not a character flaw.
tips: Write everything down rather than relying on memory
Break complex tasks into smaller, more manageable steps
Schedule cognitively demanding work for earlier in the cycle if possible
Be patient and compassionate with yourself
This resolves naturally as menstruation begins and estrogen starts rising

fields.name: low back ache
fields.description: Progesterone causes ligament laxity throughout the body including the sacroiliac and lumbar ligaments. Pelvic congestion adds to the sensation of heaviness and achiness in the lower back.
tips: Gentle lower back stretches such as child's pose and cat-cow provide relief
A heat pack on the lower back is effective
Avoid heavy lifting in this phase as ligament laxity increases injury risk
Core strengthening exercises over time reduce vulnerability to cycle-related back pain

fields.name: pelvic heaviness
fields.description: Increased blood flow to the pelvic region in preparation for potential menstruation causes a sensation of fullness, heaviness or dragging in the pelvis and lower abdomen.
tips: Lying with legs elevated can relieve the congestion sensation
Warm baths relax pelvic muscles
Gentle pelvic floor exercises improve circulation
The sensation resolves once menstruation begins

fields.name: heightened emotional sensitivity
fields.description: Falling estrogen and progesterone fluctuations affect the amygdala — the brain's emotional processing centre — increasing its reactivity to emotional stimuli. Things that would not normally be upsetting feel overwhelming.
tips: Journaling helps process and externalise intense emotions
Inform close ones about your cycle so they can offer appropriate support
Avoid making major relationship or life decisions in the late luteal phase
Physical exercise reduces amygdala reactivity
This is a physiological change, not a personality trait

fields.name: vivid dreams
fields.description: Elevated progesterone in the luteal phase is associated with more vivid, intense and emotionally charged dreaming. This is thought to be related to progesterone's effects on REM sleep architecture.
tips: Keeping a dream journal is interesting if you are curious about your patterns
This is normal and not a cause for concern
If dreams are consistently disturbing they may reflect underlying anxiety worth addressing

fields.name: sleep disturbances
fields.description: Multiple factors disrupt sleep in the luteal phase: elevated BBT, physical discomfort, anxiety, vivid dreams and progesterone-driven changes to sleep architecture including reduced REM sleep.
tips: Consistent sleep schedule is the most effective intervention
Cool bedroom temperature counteracts elevated BBT
Avoid alcohol as it reduces sleep quality
Magnesium glycinate at bedtime promotes deeper more restorative sleep

fields.name: irritability
fields.description: Falling estrogen reduces serotonin production and receptor sensitivity. Simultaneously, the neurosteroid metabolite of progesterone (allopregnanolone) fluctuates, affecting GABA and creating an irritable baseline.
tips: Regular aerobic exercise is the most effective natural serotonin booster
Avoid excess sugar and refined carbohydrates which cause blood sugar spikes and crashes
Communicate with people close to you about luteal phase changes
If irritability is severe and cyclical this may be PMDD — speak to a doctor

fields.name: tender breasts
fields.description: The combination of progesterone and estrogen stimulation causes breast tissue to swell and ducts to expand, resulting in noticeable tenderness and fullness that resolves with menstruation.
tips: Supportive bra including during sleep if severe
Reduce caffeine
Evening primrose oil at therapeutic dose
Cold compress for acute relief

fields.name: hot flashes
fields.description: Hormonal fluctuations in the late luteal phase can trigger vasomotor symptoms — sudden episodes of heat, flushing and sweating — similar to but less intense than menopausal hot flushes.
tips: Wear breathable natural fabric layers that can be removed
Keep a small fan at your desk or bedside
Avoid spicy food, alcohol and hot drinks which are common triggers
A cool cloth on the back of the neck provides rapid relief

fields.name: night sweats
fields.description: The elevated basal body temperature of the luteal phase combined with hormonal fluctuations can cause excessive sweating during sleep, disrupting sleep quality.
tips: Keep the bedroom cool — ideally 16–18°C
Use breathable cotton or moisture-wicking bedding
Avoid alcohol before bed which worsens night sweats
Keep a cool glass of water by the bed

fields.name: heart palpitations
fields.description: Hormonal fluctuations and the anxiety of the late luteal phase can cause an awareness of heartbeat or episodes of palpitations. These are typically benign but should be investigated if persistent or severe.
tips: Reduce caffeine and alcohol intake
Practice slow diaphragmatic breathing during episodes
Track whether palpitations are consistently premenstrual
Consult a doctor if palpitations are severe, prolonged or accompanied by chest pain

fields.name: dry mouth
fields.description: Progesterone can reduce saliva production in some people, causing a dry or sticky mouth sensation in the luteal phase.
tips: Stay well hydrated
Chew sugar-free gum to stimulate saliva production
Avoid alcohol and caffeine which worsen dry mouth
A water spray can provide immediate relief

fields.name: weight gain
fields.description: Water and sodium retention driven by progesterone and aldosterone cause temporary weight gain of 1–3kg in the luteal phase. This is entirely fluid, not fat, and resolves with menstruation.
tips: This is water weight, not fat — it will resolve once your period begins
Reducing salt intake moderates the degree of water retention
Weigh yourself at the same phase each cycle for meaningful comparison
Do not restrict food in response to this temporary change

fields.name: vaginal dryness
fields.description: Falling estrogen in the late luteal phase can cause reduced vaginal lubrication and mild dryness, distinct from the fertile mucus of the follicular and ovulation phases.
tips: Water-based lubricants provide immediate relief
Stay hydrated
This resolves as estrogen rises again in the next follicular phase

fields.name: painful intercourse
fields.description: Vaginal dryness and pelvic congestion in the late luteal phase can make intercourse uncomfortable or painful.
tips: Use a lubricant
Communicate with your partner
Pelvic tenderness typically resolves with menstruation

fields.name: social withdrawal
fields.description: Low energy, heightened emotional sensitivity and reduced social confidence in the late luteal phase drive a natural tendency to seek solitude and withdraw from social commitments.
tips: Gentle social plans with trusted people can be beneficial
Do not force large or demanding social events in the late luteal phase
Let close ones know how you feel rather than withdrawing without explanation
This is a healthy response to conserve energy — honour it where possible

fields.name: low self esteem
fields.description: Falling estrogen and dopamine reduce the brain's reward signalling and self-valuation mechanisms, causing a temporary but significant dip in self-confidence and self-worth.
tips: Avoid making major decisions about your body, relationships or career in this phase
Remind yourself this is a hormonal shift, not an accurate reflection of reality
Self-compassion practices and journaling help
This reliably resolves with the next cycle

fields.name: crying spells
fields.description: Rapid and significant hormone fluctuations in the late luteal phase lower the threshold for emotional responses, making crying more frequent and sometimes disproportionate to the trigger.
tips: Allow yourself to cry — suppressing it increases distress
Journaling before bed can process emotions that might otherwise surface as crying spells
Vigorous exercise is effective at discharging emotional tension
Share with trusted people rather than feeling alone with it

fields.name: overwhelm
fields.description: Reduced cognitive bandwidth, heightened emotional reactivity and physical discomfort combine in the late luteal phase to make ordinary demands feel unmanageable.
tips: Simplify your schedule and obligations during the late luteal phase
Delegate tasks where possible
Break everything into the smallest possible steps
Say no to non-essential commitments without guilt
Rest is productive in this phase

fields.name: restlessness
fields.description: Anxiety and sleep disruption in the late luteal phase create an uncomfortable inability to relax, settle or feel at ease — a restless, agitated baseline.
tips: Moderate aerobic exercise is the most effective intervention for restlessness
Mindfulness and body scan meditations help ground scattered energy
Reduce caffeine intake which amplifies restlessness
Magnesium glycinate at bedtime reduces nighttime restlessness

fields.name: difficulty making decisions
fields.description: Brain fog, emotional hyperreactivity and reduced working memory in the late luteal phase make decision-making significantly harder. Both options feel equally bad or equally good.
tips: Delay non-urgent decisions until after your period if possible
Write out a simple pros and cons list to externalise the decision
Avoid making irreversible decisions in the late luteal phase
Trust your gut for small decisions — analysis paralysis is worse now

fields.name: forgetfulness
fields.description: Estrogen is a key regulator of hippocampal memory function. Its decline in the late luteal phase measurably impairs working memory and name recall.
tips: Use reminders, lists and notes extensively during this phase
Put important items in the same place every time
Do not be hard on yourself — this is a well-documented neurological effect
Resolves with the next cycle as estrogen rises

fields.name: brain fog
fields.description: The convergence of falling estrogen, progesterone fluctuations and sleep disruption in the late luteal phase creates a subjective experience of mental cloudiness, slow processing and difficulty thinking clearly.
tips: Write things down rather than relying on working memory
Break tasks into micro-steps
Adequate sleep is the single most important factor in reducing brain fog
Omega-3 fatty acids support neurological function
Be patient — this is temporary and resolves with the cycle

fields.name: memory issues
fields.description: Estrogen supports long-term memory encoding in the hippocampus. Its decline in the late luteal phase measurably reduces memory consolidation and verbal recall.
tips: Use reminders and calendar alerts for important tasks
Spaced repetition learning is less effective in this phase
Avoid self-criticism — this is physiological not a sign of cognitive decline

fields.name: poor coordination
fields.description: Progesterone and its neurosteroid metabolites can subtly affect motor control, proprioception and spatial awareness in the late luteal phase.
tips: Take extra care with tasks requiring fine motor precision
Avoid high-risk activities requiring sharp coordination if feeling significantly affected
Rest if needed

fields.name: clumsiness
fields.description: Hormonal effects on spatial processing and motor coordination make people more prone to dropping things, bumping into objects and misjudging distances in the late luteal phase.
tips: Take your time with physical tasks
Be extra careful in the kitchen or with fragile items
Acknowledge this as a known luteal phase phenomenon rather than personal ineptitude

fields.name: slow reaction time
fields.description: Progesterone's sedative neurological effect and the cognitive fog of the late luteal phase measurably slow reaction time and processing speed.
tips: Avoid driving when very fatigued in the late luteal phase
Avoid high-speed or high-precision tasks if significantly affected
Rest improves reaction time more than pushing through

fields.name: excessive appetite
fields.description: Falling serotonin and progesterone's effect on appetite-regulating hormones increase hunger, particularly for calorie-dense carbohydrate-rich foods. Metabolic rate is also slightly elevated in the luteal phase.
tips: Eat protein and fat-rich meals which are more satiating and don't spike blood sugar
Do not severely restrict food — this worsens cravings and mood
Plan nutritious satisfying snacks in advance
The metabolic rate is genuinely elevated so needing more food is physiological

fields.name: sugar cravings
fields.description: Low serotonin in the late luteal phase drives specific cravings for sugar and refined carbohydrates, which cause a rapid but transient serotonin boost via insulin-driven tryptophan uptake.
tips: Complex carbohydrates (oats, wholegrains, sweet potato) provide a gentler, more sustained serotonin response
Dark chocolate satisfies sweet cravings while providing magnesium and antioxidants
Magnesium supplementation reduces cravings over multiple cycles
Eating protein with every meal blunts blood sugar spikes that worsen cravings

fields.name: salt cravings
fields.description: Aldosterone-driven sodium retention paradoxically increases salt cravings. Progesterone also increases the perception of salt taste making salty foods more rewarding.
tips: Opt for healthier salty options such as nuts, seeds and olives rather than crisps and processed food
Be mindful that excess sodium will worsen water retention and bloating
A small amount of salt is fine — severe restriction is not necessary or helpful

fields.name: caffeine sensitivity
fields.description: Hormonal fluctuations in the luteal phase alter the metabolism of caffeine and increase neurological sensitivity to its stimulant effects, making the same dose feel stronger and more anxiety-provoking.
tips: Reduce caffeine intake to one cup per day or switch to half-caf
Avoid caffeine after midday as it disrupts already-fragile sleep
Herbal teas such as chamomile, lemon balm and passionflower are calming alternatives

fields.name: alcohol sensitivity
fields.description: Hormonal fluctuations in the luteal phase alter liver enzyme activity and neurotransmitter sensitivity, making alcohol feel stronger, worsen mood more severely and cause worse hangovers.
tips: Reduce alcohol intake significantly in the late luteal phase
Alcohol worsens anxiety, sleep quality and depressive symptoms in this phase
Alternate with sparkling water if at social events

fields.name: alcohol cravings
fields.description: Low mood, anxiety and social discomfort in the late luteal phase can trigger cravings for alcohol as a self-medication for psychological distress.
tips: Exercise is a more effective and durable mood booster than alcohol
Identify the underlying feeling driving the craving — often anxiety or low mood
Speak to a doctor if alcohol use feels difficult to manage in a cyclical pattern

fields.name: skin changes
fields.description: Progesterone increases sebum production, enlarges pores and increases skin's inflammatory response making the luteal phase the most acne-prone part of the cycle.
tips: Adjust skincare to include more salicylic acid products in the luteal phase
Use non-comedogenic makeup and skincare
Avoid picking at skin which worsens inflammation and scarring

fields.name: hair loss
fields.description: Rapid hormonal fluctuations, iron depletion from menstrual blood loss and stress can trigger telogen effluvium — a temporary increase in hair shedding noticed most during washing or brushing.
tips: Eat iron-rich and protein-rich foods to support hair follicle health
Gentle hair care and avoiding tight styles reduces mechanical hair loss
Consult a doctor if shedding is severe — a blood test can identify nutritional deficiencies

fields.name: nail changes
fields.description: Hormonal fluctuations and nutritional changes around menstruation can cause nails to become temporarily more brittle, soft or prone to peeling.
tips: Use a nail strengthening treatment
Eat protein and biotin-rich foods (eggs, nuts, seeds)
Keep nails short to prevent breakage during this phase

fields.name: cold sensitivity
fields.description: Blood loss reduces hemoglobin and impairs circulation, leading to increased sensitivity to cold particularly in the hands and feet. Low iron compounds this effect.
tips: Layer clothing for easy temperature regulation
Warm drinks and soups help maintain core temperature
Iron-rich foods improve oxygen-carrying capacity and circulation over time
Thermal gloves and socks help during particularly cold weather

fields.name: missed period
fields.description: Menstruation has not started by the expected date. Causes range from pregnancy to stress, thyroid dysfunction, PCOS, extreme weight changes, and excessive exercise. Defined clinically as late after 5 days past expected start.
tips: Take a pregnancy test if sexually active and period is more than 5 days late
Track how many days late for useful information for a doctor
Consult a GP if periods are consistently late or absent for more than 3 months
Stress is the most common benign cause of a late period

fields.name: cycle delay anxiety
fields.description: Uncertainty about the cause of a delayed period generates significant anxiety, which itself can further suppress ovulation and delay the period creating a self-perpetuating cycle.
tips: Taking a pregnancy test removes the uncertainty that fuels anxiety
Stress is the most common non-pathological cause of a late period
Track other possible causes: travel, illness, significant life stress, changes in weight or exercise
Speak to a doctor if late periods are a recurring pattern

fields.name: continued PMS symptoms
fields.description: When the period is late, luteal phase symptoms such as bloating, breast tenderness, mood changes and fatigue continue beyond the expected start date as progesterone is maintained.
tips: Treat symptoms as you would normal PMS
They should resolve once menstruation begins
If symptoms are severe and prolonged speak to a doctor

fields.name: increased urination
fields.description: Rising hCG in early pregnancy stimulates increased renal blood flow and kidney filtration rate, causing noticeably more frequent urination before a missed period is even confirmed.
tips: Take a pregnancy test if this symptom is new and combined with a late period
Can also be caused by increased water intake, caffeine, or a urinary tract infection
UTI should be considered if urination is painful or burning

fields.name: spotting
fields.description: Light pink or brown spotting occurring when a period is late may represent implantation bleeding (6–12 days post-conception) or spotting from an anovulatory cycle where no ovulation occurred.
tips: Implantation bleeding is considerably lighter and shorter than a normal period
Take a pregnancy test 3–4 days after spotting if a period is overdue
Brown spotting indicates old blood and is not an emergency
Consult a doctor if spotting is heavy or prolonged

fields.name: pelvic cramping without bleeding
fields.description: The uterus may begin contracting in preparation for menstruation even when the period is delayed. Cramping without bleeding is common in the days immediately before a late period arrives.
tips: A heating pad provides comfort
Ibuprofen helps if cramps are significant
If cramping is severe without any bleeding and a pregnancy test is positive seek medical attention — this can indicate an ectopic pregnancy

fields.name: heightened smell sensitivity
fields.description: A sudden and significant increase in sensitivity to smells is a well-recognised early pregnancy sign, thought to be caused by rapidly rising estrogen and hCG. Common triggers are cooking smells, perfumes and cigarette smoke.
tips: Take a pregnancy test if this symptom is new and combined with a late period
Avoid known trigger smells
Fresh air and cool environments help with smell-triggered nausea
Peppermint and lemon scents are often well tolerated and can counteract nausea

fields.name: food aversions
fields.description: Strong aversions to previously tolerated foods, particularly strong-smelling or fatty foods, are an early pregnancy sign caused by rising hCG and estrogen affecting the olfactory and taste systems.
tips: Eat whatever you can tolerate without forcing foods that trigger aversion
Cold foods are often better tolerated than hot foods as they release fewer odour molecules
Take a pregnancy test if food aversions are new and you have a late period

fields.name: breast sensitivity
fields.description: Continued elevated progesterone from an extended luteal phase or the rapid rise of hCG in early pregnancy maintains and often intensifies breast sensitivity and fullness beyond the expected period.
tips: A well-fitting supportive bra helps
Persistent and worsening breast tenderness alongside a late period may indicate early pregnancy
Tenderness from an extended luteal phase resolves once menstruation begins

fields.name: prolonged luteal symptoms
fields.description: When the luteal phase extends beyond its usual 14 days, all progesterone-driven symptoms including bloating, mood changes, sleep disruption and fatigue continue and may intensify.
tips: Treat as you would normal PMS symptoms
Should resolve once menstruation begins
If the luteal phase is consistently long speak to a doctor about progesterone levels

fields.name: early pregnancy nausea
fields.description: Rising hCG levels, often doubling every 48 hours in early pregnancy, are the primary driver of nausea. Contrary to the name, this can occur at any time of day. Typically starts around 6 weeks after the last period.
tips: Small frequent meals prevent an empty stomach which worsens nausea
Ginger in all forms (tea, biscuits, capsules) has the best evidence for pregnancy nausea
Cold and bland foods are generally better tolerated
Vitamin B6 supplementation has evidence for morning sickness
Seek medical attention if vomiting prevents any food or fluid intake

fields.name: prolonged fatigue
fields.medical_term: early pregnancy or luteal phase fatigue
fields.description: Continued elevated progesterone maintains its sedative effect. In early pregnancy, rising hCG and the significant metabolic demands of implantation cause profound fatigue even before a positive test.
tips: Rest as much as circumstances allow — this fatigue is physiological and significant
Iron and B12 levels are worth checking if fatigue is severe
Light walking is energising but avoid high-intensity exercise if very fatigued
Small protein-rich snacks every 2–3 hours maintain blood sugar and energy

fields.name: implantation spotting
fields.description: When a fertilised embryo implants into the uterine lining, a small amount of bleeding can occur 6–12 days after conception. This appears as very light pink or brown spotting lasting 1–2 days, significantly lighter than a normal period.
tips: Implantation bleeding is much lighter and shorter than a period
It occurs around the time a period might be expected which can cause confusion
Take a pregnancy test 3–4 days after noticing spotting if a period is overdue
Brown spotting is old blood and is not a cause for alarm

fields.name: anovulatory cramping
fields.description: In cycles where ovulation does not occur (anovulatory cycles), the absence of progesterone following normal luteal phase development can cause irregular cramping without the usual cyclical pattern.
tips: Heating pad provides comfort
Consult a doctor if anovulatory cycles are suspected to recur — PCOS and thyroid disorders can cause this
Tracking cycle patterns over time helps identify anovulatory cycles

fields.name: hyperosmia
fields.description: A clinically recognised heightening of olfactory sensitivity, hyperosmia in the context of a late period is considered an early pregnancy indicator. The mechanism involves estrogen's effect on olfactory neurons.
tips: Take a pregnancy test if this is new and combined with a late period
Avoid known trigger smells
Fresh air helps
Peppermint scent is often well tolerated

fields.name: metallic taste
fields.description: A persistent metallic or bitter taste in the mouth is a commonly reported early pregnancy symptom caused by rising estrogen and hCG altering taste receptor sensitivity.
tips: Drinking water frequently helps
Citrus flavours (lemon water, citrus gum) neutralise the metallic taste effectively
Cold foods have less taste intensity than hot foods
Take a pregnancy test if this is new alongside a late period

fields.name: frequent urination
fields.description: Early pregnancy increases blood volume and renal filtration rate, causing noticeably increased urinary frequency even before a period is significantly late.
tips: Stay hydrated despite the inconvenience
Rule out UTI if urination is painful, burning or bloody
Take a pregnancy test if combined with a late period

fields.name: urinary urgency
fields.description: Pelvic congestion from an extended luteal phase or early pregnancy can create pressure on the bladder causing a sudden urgent need to urinate.
tips: Pelvic floor exercises (Kegels) improve bladder control over time
Consult a doctor if urgency is new and persistent
Rule out UTI

fields.name: excessive thirst
fields.description: Early pregnancy increases blood volume requiring more fluid intake. Rising hCG also affects fluid regulation signalling, increasing thirst.
tips: Stay hydrated
Monitor if accompanied by frequent urination and fatigue — together these warrant a pregnancy test and possibly a blood glucose check

fields.name: low mood late
fields.description: The uncertainty of a late period, continued hormonal imbalance and extended PMS symptoms compound to create significant low mood when a period is overdue.
tips: Address the uncertainty directly by taking a pregnancy test
Reach out to a trusted friend or family member
Consult a doctor if late periods and low mood are a recurring cyclical pattern — PMDD is treatable
